pages.t: AD Penalty Clause in Nepal's PPA — Research Compilation
pages.e: Research compilation on the Available Declaration (AD) penalty mechanism in Nepal's standard hydropower PPA. Based on direct PPA text analysis by Parajuli (2023) and ERC regulatory commentary.
ADB assessment of Nepal's hydropower development trajectory, covering growth patterns, project pipeline, and development constraints.

pages.t: AEPC Renewable Framework and Progress Reports
pages.e: Primary source for Nepal's off-grid and distributed solar story. AEPC ("Alternative Energy Promotion Centre") is the government institution responsible for rural electrification through renewables, standing alongside (not inside) NEA. It is the operator and financier of the solar
Reference compilation for the 900 MW Arun-3 Hydroelectric Project — combining the 2014 Project Development Agreement (PDA) between Nepal and SJVN with 2024–2025 construction progress reports.

pages.t: CARE Ratings Nepal — Sanima Mai Hydropower Credit Assessments
pages.e: CARE Ratings Nepal surveillance reports and rating rationales for Sanima Mai Hydropower Limited, a 22 MW private IPP on the Mai Khola (eastern Nepal). The Sanima Mai generation data provides one of the most complete multi-year time series for tracking Q-design discharge performan

pages.t: Chilime Hydropower Company Limited Annual Report FY 2078/79
pages.e: The Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL) annual report for fiscal year 2078/79 provides the verified primary-source data for Chilime's dual-tier PPA billing architecture and generation performance. Chilime is an NEA subsidiary (NEA retains 51% controlling stake), and its PPA

pages.t: DoED Licensing Directive 2075 — Hydropower Project Licensing
pages.e: "विद्युत आयोजनाको अनुमतिपत्र सम्बन्धी निर्देशिका, २०७५" (Directive on Licensing of Electricity Projects, 2075 BS), issued by the Department of Electricity Development (DoED) on Kartik 7, 2075 (October 2018).

pages.t: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record
pages.e: Reference compilation for the Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project, covering the original 1980s ADB-supported feasibility, the 2021 restart with land acquisition, the 2024 NEA construction proposal to government, and the 2024–2025 financing conversations with AIIB.

pages.t: EIB Promotion of Solar Energy in Rural and Semi-Urban Regions II
pages.e: European Investment Bank line of up to EUR 8 million for the development of ground-mounted, grid-connected solar PV installations at four locations in Nepal. Implemented through the Government of Nepal with AEPC-facilitated site selection and NEA-grid interconnection.

The primary satellite-derived solar resource dataset for Nepal's explorer map layer and the reference cross-check for every GHI / DNI number used elsewhere in the wiki. Provided as a free-to-use web-tile and downloadable-data service by Solargis under the World Bank Group ESMAP p
Strategic roadmap for producing green hydrogen from Nepal's surplus monsoon hydropower via electrolysis. Envisions an entirely new domestic industrial ecosystem absorbing stranded wet-season power.
Regional glacier change assessment for the Hindu Kush Himalaya. Documents the scale and pace of glacier retreat affecting Nepal's river systems and long-term hydropower viability.
ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system.

pages.t: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin
pages.e: Detailed assessment of sediment dynamics, erosion, and environmental threats in the koshi-basin. Provides the evidence base for treating sediment as a central engineering and planning constraint.

pages.t: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018)
pages.e: India's regulatory framework governing cross-border electricity trade. Defines approval mechanisms, designated authorities, and conditions for Nepal's electricity exports to India. Critically, includes restrictions on projects with certain foreign investment.

pages.t: IRENA Renewable Readiness Assessment and REmap Nepal
pages.e: Primary international source for solar and renewable cost benchmarking against Nepal specifics. Two IRENA document streams are relevant:

This is the Annexes volume (Vol. 2) of the JICA Study Team's Final Report for the (IPSDP) — the same study whose compact policy conclusions are captured in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025. The executive summary is where the headline numbers and milestone ladder live; this volume i

pages.t: Kali Gandaki A — ADB Performance Evaluation & Wikipedia
pages.e: Reference compilation for the 144 MW Kali Gandaki A plant at Mirmi, Syangja — combining the ADB's post-project performance evaluation (the plant was ADB/JBIC co-financed) with the Wikipedia-aggregated operational history and the recent NEA rehabilitation tender.

pages.t: Khimti-I Project Research — PPA, Generation, Finance, Ownership
pages.e: Research compilation covering Nepal's first large private IPP: 60 MW Khimti-I on the Khimti Khola. Compiled from Himal Power Limited disclosures, Statkraft press releases, and PPA analysis.

Comprehensive research compilation covering the 213 MW Likhu hydropower cascade (Likhu-1, Likhu-2, Likhu-4, Likhu Khola A). Compiled from CARE Ratings Nepal issuer ratings, developer disclosures, and NEA transmission data.

This executive summary is the clearest compact statement of the current MoEWRI/JICA planning logic for Nepal's long-horizon power buildout through 2040. It ties together demand growth, hydropower sequencing, 400 kV backbone and interconnection expansion, export strategy, and fina

pages.t: Mugu Karnali Storage — Feasibility, Bids & EOI Record (2024–2025)
pages.e: Reference compilation for the 1,902 MW Mugu Karnali Storage Hydroelectric Project — Nepal's largest proposed generation asset, in pre-feasibility under Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUC) and open to public–private partnership bidding.
Older but widely cited national planning document for Nepal's water resources. Provides the foundational estimates of Nepal's contribution to the Ganges system and total annual runoff.

The most current official snapshot of Nepal's electricity system. Covers installed capacity, generation mix, trade volumes, grid infrastructure, and project pipeline for fiscal year 2024/25 (Nepali FY 2081/82).

Official Nepal Electricity Authority annual-book publication for FY 2019/20 (B.S. 2077). The volume mixes directorate narrative, project status notes, site/substation photographs, appendix tables, and map pages. For the wiki, its highest-value material is in the transmission chap

pages.t: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025)
pages.e: Reference compilation for Nepal's current and planned high-voltage transmission assets — the 400 kV east–west backbone and parallel central corridor, the legacy 220 kV and 132 kV grid, and the cross-border interconnections with India (ten links at five voltage levels). Drawn from

pages.t: "Investigative Report: PPA Data Retrieval from ICRA & CARE Ratings Nepal"
pages.e: Source type: Research compilation — Google Docs export (15 pages) Original: Mission-based financial investigation sourced from ICRA Nepal surveillance reports (A.996, B.119), CARE Ratings Nepal rating rationales, and statutory filings.

pages.t: "Mission 1: Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) — Benchmark IPP Success"
pages.e: Source type: Research compilation — single-project deep-dive (4 pages) Original: Forensic reconstruction of Sahas Urja Limited sourced from ICRA Nepal surveillance reports (A.996, B.119) and corporate disclosures.

pages.t: "Systemic Risk & Triple-Authority Conflict in Nepal's Hydropower Sector"
pages.e: Source type: Research compilation — Google Docs export (17 pages) Original: Exhaustive investigation of structural imbalances at the intersection of capital markets, regulatory frameworks, and project finance in Nepal's hydropower IPP sector.

Reference compilation for the 140 MW Tanahu Hydropower Project's construction progress and social-compliance posture, drawn from ADB's own project monitoring reports and two Accountability Mechanism complaints (2024 and 2025).

pages.t: UKHLL Annual Reports — Upper Tamakoshi Hydropower Limited
pages.e: The audited annual reports of Upper Tamakoshi Hydropower Limited (UKHLL), the NEA subsidiary that owns and operates the 456 MW Upper Tamakoshi Hydroelectric Project — Nepal's largest operating hydropower plant. The reports are available at utkhpl.org.np and provide the primary-so

pages.t: UKHLL Financials and Generation Data — FY 2079/80 to FY 2081/82
pages.e: Comprehensive research compilation covering Upper Tamakoshi Hydroelectric Limited (UKHLL) financial and operational data for FY 2079/80 through FY 2081/82. Compiled from NEA Annual Reports, UKHLL audited accounts, ICRA Nepal credit assessments, and ShareSansar disclosures.

pages.t: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record
pages.e: Reference compilation for the 900 MW Upper Karnali Hydroelectric Project — GMR-sponsored, repeatedly PDA-extended, and the canonical example of a stalled India-developed export project in Nepal.

pages.t: Urja Khabar Generation Audit — IPP Generation vs Contracted
pages.e: Clarification of the widely-cited Urja Khabar generation audit. The source is not an independent Urja Khabar dataset or audit report — it is a January 2026 Urja Khabar magazine article by Kumar Pandey citing a MoEWRI report from Ashad 11, 2077 BS (mid-2020) on "financially distre

pages.t: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025)
pages.e: The World Bank's comprehensive economic assessment of Nepal with a significant energy/hydropower chapter. Provides the strongest international-institution perspective on Nepal's energy transition, trade patterns, and system constraints.

pages.t: World Bank ESMAP Nepal Solar and Wind Resource Measurement
pages.e: The ground-measured solar and wind resource dataset for Nepal. Operated 2015–2019 under the World Bank ESMAP Renewable Energy Resource Mapping & Geospatial Planning programme, implemented on the ground through AEPC with technical delivery by Vaisala / 3TIER (solar) and DNV GL (wi

pages.t: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment
pages.e: Regional-scale analysis of the Ganges basin covering water storage, flood management, and transboundary dynamics. Provides the valley-shape storage paradox that Public source review highlighted.

pages.t: World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project
pages.e: The institutional anchor for Nepal's first utility-scale, grid-tied solar farm and the financing channel that broke Nepal's "can we actually build solar at utility scale?" learning curve. IDA credit to the Government of Nepal, implemented by NEA under a World Bank project supervi

pages.t: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022)
pages.e: Policy-oriented assessment of Nepal's power sector institutions and reform needs. Used as part of the source base for hydropower potential labeling and institutional analysis.

pages.t: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic
pages.e: A water-sector-wide diagnostic covering storage requirements, irrigation, and the intersection of water management with energy. Provides the most authoritative estimate of Nepal's seasonal storage gap.

pages.t: WECS/DHM Methodology — Hydrological Estimation for Ungauged Locations (1990)
pages.e: "Methodology for Estimating Hydrological Characteristics of Ungauged Locations in Nepal" (WECS/DHM, 1990, Seq. No. 331, Ministry of Water Resources). The regulatory default methodology for feasibility studies on Nepal's vast number of projects on ungauged tributaries.

Covers Nepal's full energy balance for FY 2079/80 (~2022/23), including all energy sources (not just electricity). Provides the broader context for where grid electricity fits within Nepal's total energy system. This is the authoritative denominator for every "hydro share of tota

pages.t: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019)
pages.e: Site Inspection during Safeguards

pages.t: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024)
pages.e: The 2024 master planning document for Nepal's river basins. Provides basin-level discharge data, seasonal flow profiles, hydropower potential assessments, and strategic planning frameworks. This is the most authoritative recent basin-level reference.

Government of Nepal institution responsible for rural electrification through renewables. Technically a semi-autonomous body under the Ministry of Energy, Water Resources & Irrigation (MoEWRI); operationally the country's primary administrator of the off-grid solar, biogas, impro

Andhi Khola is a small operating project in Syangja, but it is pinned in both the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-storage-shortlist because the same river/site family appears in JICA's promising-storage comparison. It is a useful reminder that storage value is not th

Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut Company Limited (AKJCL) is a 42.9 MW run-of-river hydropower project. Beyond its generation capacity, Ankhu Khola is a textbook example of the hydropower rights-share bailout pattern documented in Nepal's capital markets.
Figure 6.3-25 River Longitudinal Profile of the Arun River
Figure 6.3-32 River Longitudinal Profile of the Bagmati River
Figure 6.3-23 River Longitudinal Profile of the Balephi River
Nepal's first electricity export to a country other than India. A trilateral arrangement routed through India's grid — making India simultaneously Nepal's competitor, buyer, and logistics gatekeeper.
Sub-10 MW run-of-river hydropower project, operational. This page exists as the wiki's primary audited case study for the nea-triple-authority conflict.

Betan Karnali is a large semi-reservoir hydropower project on the Karnali, historically carried in the registry as a 688 MW survey-stage project and more recently advanced in public reporting as a 439 MW generation-licence / investment-approval project. It is pinned in the data-l

Bheri-1 HEP is a 440 MW survey-stage project on the Bheri in Rukum West, promoted in the registry by Gezhouba Group Power Investment Nepal Pvt. Ltd. It is not on the priority watchlist, but it is pinned by the data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects layer because it is one of the ten
256 MW · survey / study · on the Bheri · in Jajarkot district.
300 MW · survey / study · on the Bheri · in Jajarkot district.

A planned 400 kV evacuation corridor for the western Bheri system, centred in the annual book on uttarganga-storage and the wider Sani Bheri IPP pipeline. In 2020 it was still a land, survey, and substation preparation story rather than a built line.
Bhutan is the most frequent regional comparison for Nepal's hydropower trajectory. The comparison is instructive and somewhat brutal.

Nepal's first utility-scale NEA-EPC, NEA-owned, NEA-operated solar plant. 25 MWp ground-mounted grid-tied PV at Bidur, Nuwakot district, commissioned 2022 under the World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project. Interconnected to the Trishuli 33 kV grid.

pages.t: Bishnu Priya Solar Farm (Surya Power, ~1 MWp)
pages.e: Small demonstration-scale IPP solar plant developed by Surya Power Company Pvt. Ltd., commissioned ~2018 in Nawalparasi district, Lumbini Province. The earliest Phase I IPP plant to reach COD and historically significant as Nepal's first grid-tied commercial IPP solar plant.
260 MW · survey / study · on the Budhigandaki · in Gorkha district.
420 MW · survey / study · on the Budhi Gandaki · in Gorkha district.
Figure 6.3-17 River Longitudinal Profile of the Budhi Gandaki River

Early-round IPP utility solar plant developed by Ridi Hydropower Development Co. Ltd., commissioned ~2020 under the 5-developer 24 MWp competitive-bid round that constituted Nepal's first utility-scale solar IPP cycle.
Powerhouse Area of Chainpur Seti
The operational far-west north-south 132 kV line connecting the Chameliya hydropower area through Syaule into the Attariya grid node.

30 MW Peaking Run-of-River (PROR) project on the Chameliya River, Darchula District, Sudurpashchim Province. Directly owned and operated by the Nepal Electricity Authority — not an independent corporate entity. This page exists as the wiki's primary case study in state capital in

The planned far-western Nepal–India interconnection, routed from Chameliya (Darchula, Nepal) to Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand). This would be the first 220 kV interconnection in the Mahakali basin and the gateway link for any future hydropower expansion in far-western Nepal
Chera-1 is a 149 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Karnali basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist.

This page currently records cover-photo evidence rather than a full technical corridor brief. On the contents page of nea-annual-report-fy2024-25, NEA explicitly labels the annual report's front cover image as Chilime-Trishuli 220 kV TL.
Under construction powerhouse filled with the water and debris during the flood.

The eastern 132 kV evacuation chain that the annual book describes in three stages: Damak–Godak, Godak–Phidim, and onward Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli). It is the practical corridor through which the Mai/Kabeli-side hill projects were being tied back into the national system.

The west-central hydropower evacuation corridor — running from the upper Kali Gandaki valley (Dana, Kushma, Parbat) down to New Butwal. Built to pick up the cluster of IPPs in the Modi and Kaligandaki tributaries and tie them into the growing western grid.

Nepal's first and — at the time of writing — only operational 400 kV cross-border link. Every exported MWh, including the bangladesh-trade-route allocation, transits this line. It is the linchpin of Nepal's current export strategy.
KC-3: Transmission Line Stretch from Basantapur
27 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Dordi · in Lamjung district.
Figure 6.3-20 River Longitudinal Profile of the Dudh Koshi River
25 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Myagdi Khola · in Myagdi district.

The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagiri drops >5,500 verti

Nepal's second 400 kV cross-border link, under construction. When commissioned it will double Nepal's 400 kV export capacity and break the single-point-of-failure dependency on dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. It is the direct complement of the mca-central-400 corridor, which terminates at

The 220 kV east–west spine across central Nepal, running through the Hetauda → Bharatpur → Bardaghat load corridor. This is the line that carried Nepal's grid before the 400 kV era — and still carries the majority of Terai distribution load.
220 kV Substation at Dhalkebar
235 MW · survey / study · on the Humla Karnali · in Humla district.
335 MW · survey / study · on the Humla Karnali · in Humla district.

The planned third 400 kV Nepal–India link, critical to evacuating the arun-3 export block (900 MW) and later upper-karnali-tier eastern generation. Currently in implementation-setup phase — the route exists on paper and in the bilateral MoU record, but construction has not begun.
Energy Balance (GWh) in FY 2076/77 (2019/20)
307 MW · survey / study · on the Bheri · in Jajarkot district.

The Kabeli 132 kV corridor is an eastern Nepal branch network around the Soyak-Godak-Amarpur/Phidim area. It is represented as a branch corridor because the recovered NEA Kabeli IEE describes a bifurcated route rather than a single linear line.
37.6 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Kabeli Khola · in Taplejung district.
25 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Kabeli Khola · in Taplejung district.

Nepal's largest operating power plant of any kind (144 MW), the Kali Gandaki A run-of-river scheme at Mirmi has anchored the national grid since 2002. It is Nepali-owned (NEA), ADB/JBIC-financed, and a useful foil to the newer India-developed export projects: it shows what a mult
Kali Gandaki-Kowan is a 400 MW hydropower project under construction on the Kali Gandaki River — one of the largest projects in Nepal's active construction pipeline.
65 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Kali Gandaki · in Myagdi district.

The Karnali basin is Nepal's western major river system and the largest by area at ~43,153 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Bheri, West Seti, Humla Karnali, Mugu Karnali, and Tila. The Karnali feeds the Ghaghara, which contributes ~40% of the Ganges' baseflow at Varanasi

The largest single hydropower project ever proposed for Nepal at 10,800 MW — an order of magnitude beyond anything operating or under construction. Located on the karnali-basin main stem near the Chisapani gorge in the western Terai, this is the project that would transform Nepal

A programme line referenced in the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25 (Distribution / Customer Service Directorate) covering off-grid solar plants with Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in Karnali, with operation and maintenance support from NEA. The programme is emerging as Nepal'
45 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Kasuwa · in Sankhuwasabha district.

The oldest Nepal–India interconnection still in operation — the historic first link that defined the Nepal–India power trade template. Originally energised in 1971 at 33 kV, upgraded to 132 kV, it has been through five decades of bilateral tariff and exchange-rate politics.

NEA's Kathmandu Valley underground-distribution program as it appeared in the FY 2019/20 annual book: a public-facing modernization project aimed at moving parts of the capital's medium- and low-voltage network underground while expanding associated urban distribution capacity.
40 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Khani Khola · in Dolakha district.
30 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Khani Khola · in Dolakha district.
24.1 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Khare · in Dolakha district.

The Khimti Khola in Dolakha district is one of Nepal's earlier IPP-developed hydropower corridors. The cascade includes multiple projects at different stages, with the original Khimti-I plant serving as one of the country's first large private hydropower investments.

The original "IPP-to-grid" line in Nepal. Built to evacuate khimti-i — the country's first large IPP (1996 PPA, 2000 commissioning) — toward the central grid at Dhalkebar. Its history mirrors the birth of private hydropower in Nepal and the long legal and political battles that s
60 MW · operating · on the Khimti Khola · in Dolakha and Ramechhap districts. Nepal's first large private IPP. Commissioned July 2000 under a January 15, 1996 BOOT PPA.
48.8 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Khimti Khola · in Dolakha district.
This page currently records back-cover evidence from nea-annual-report-fy2024-25. On the report's contents page, NEA labels the upper back-cover photo as Khudi 220/132 kV Substation.

Planned 50 MW solar project at Khungri, Pyuthan district, designed to integrate into the Khungri substation adjacent to Madi Khola and Lungri Khola hydropower. The first at-scale solar-hydro hybrid (Archetype 1) configuration in Nepal, referenced in the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/

Kimathanka Arun HEP is a 450 MW survey-stage project on the Arun in Sankhuwasabha. It is pinned in data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is the eighth-largest marker in the display set and part of the same Arun corridor that makes arun-3 nationally important.
The under-construction 132 kV line extending the western grid north from Kohalpur toward Surkhet and Dailekh.
Kokhajor-1 is a 111.5 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Koshi basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it has a high dry-energy share for a relatively small project.

The Koshi (Sapta Koshi) basin is Nepal's eastern major river system. It drains ~27,818 km² within Nepal through seven major tributaries, including the Arun, Tamor, and Sunkoshi. The Arun is one of Nepal's oldest rivers — older than the Himalayas themselves, predating the mountain
Repair works on draft tube during overhauling
60 MW · operating · on the Kulekhani · in Makawanpur district.
32 MW · operating · on the Kulekhani · in Makawanpur district.
14 MW · operating · on the Kulekhani · in Makwanpur district.
310 MW · survey / study · on the Langtang · in Rasuwa district.
160 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Lapche · in Dolakha district.
77 MW · operating · on the Likhu Khola · in Solukhumbu, Okhaldhunga, and Ramechhap districts. The largest plant in the Likhu Cascade (213 MW combined across 4 plants).
55 MW · operating · on the Likhu Khola · in Ramechhap and Okhaldhunga districts. Part of the Likhu Cascade (213 MW combined across 4 plants).

52.4 MW · operating · on the Likhu Khola · in Ramechhap district. Originally designed as a 120 MW export-oriented project, completely redesigned and downscaled after a cross-border transmission dispute. Part of the Likhu Cascade (213 MW combined).

The Likhu Khola is a hydropower-rich tributary in the koshi-basin, straddling Solukhumbu and Ramechhap districts. The Likhu system has attracted significant IPP investment with multiple generation-licence projects across the upper and lower reaches.
29.04 MW · operating · on the Likhu Khola · in Solukhumbu district. The smallest plant in the Likhu Cascade (213.44 MW combined across 4 plants) and the first MV Dugar project to commission.

Lower Badigad is a 380.3 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Gandaki basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it pairs large carryover storage with meaningful dry-season output, making it more relevant to system value than many larger wet-season-heavy pr
21.6 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Hewa Khola · in Panchthar district.

Lower Jhimruk is a 142.5 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist as a medium-basin storage option, with the important caveat that its layout conflicts with Naumure in the current design set.
28.1 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Likhu Khola · in Ramechhap district.
20 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Modi Khola · in Parbat district.

Lower Solu is an 82 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction in the Solukhumbu district — part of the Dudh Koshi river system that includes the benchmark Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) project.

Madhya Bhotekoshi (Middle Bhotekoshi) is a 102 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Sindhupalchok district. It is one of the key capacity additions in the Bhote Koshi corridor, an area that has seen repeated geological setbacks includi
Madhya Marsyangdi (Middle Marsyangdi) is a 70 MW operating hydropower project on the Marsyangdi River — one of Nepal's established generation assets with a multi-decade operational track record.

Madi is a 199.8 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because its dry-energy ratio is useful even though it is not a headline-MW project.

The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multipurpose project and t
22 MW · operating · on the Mai Khola · in Ilam district.
NEA's main system-operations node in Kathmandu, paired in the annual book with the Emergency Control Center at Hetauda as the backup facility for national grid dispatch and contingency management.
This page currently records back-cover evidence from nea-annual-report-fy2024-25. On the report's contents page, NEA labels the lower back-cover photo as Markichowk 220/132 kV Substation.
50 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Marsyangdi · in Lamjung district.

The Marsyangdi upper 220 kV corridor is the upstream evacuation path from the Manang/upper Marsyangdi area south through Khudi and Besishahar toward Udipur. It matters because western and central hydropower build-out depends on moving generation out of the Marsyangdi basin withou
Figure 6.3-16 River Longitudinal Profile of the Marsyangdi River

The second 400 kV spine Nepal is building — the Millennium Challenge Account (MCA-Nepal) compact's headline infrastructure, running from Lapsiphedi (Kathmandu Valley ring) through Ratmate, Hetauda, Damauli and terminating at New Butwal, where it feeds the gorakhpur-butwal-interco
50 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Mewa Khola · in Taplejung district.
54 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Tamor · in Taplejung district.

pages.t: Mithila Solar PV Electric Project (Eco Power, 10 MWp)
pages.e: Early-round IPP utility solar plant developed by Eco Power Development Company Pvt. Ltd., commissioned ~2021 in Dhanusha district, Madhesh Province. The largest single Phase I IPP plant by MWp.

A western-hill 132 kV evacuation project aimed at improving supply in the Pokhara/Lekhnath zone while collecting power from the Modi-side hydropower cluster. In the annual-book snapshot, the associated Lahachowk switching and substation works were still under construction.
42 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Mristi · in Myagdi district.

At a proposed 1,902 MW, Mugu Karnali would be the single largest generation asset Nepal has ever planned — by itself larger than the country's entire current operating hydropower fleet. Still at pre-feasibility stage under state-owned Vidhyut Utpadan Company (VUCL), open to publi

Not a project, an entity classification of the geographic zone with Nepal's best solar resource. Covers the trans-Himalayan rain-shadow valleys of Mustang, Dolpa, upper Manang, upper Humla, Mugu (upper), and parts of Jumla — a band of high-elevation (2,500–4,500 m) valleys shield

Nalsyau Gad is a 410 MW storage candidate in the Karnali basin and the best dry-energy performer in the JICA promising-storage set used by this wiki. It is pinned in both data-layer-storage-shortlist and data-layer-priority-watchlist because it contributes the kind of dry-season

Naumure is a 245 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because its effective storage is unusually large for its installed capacity, giving it strong seasonal-regulation value in a water-deficit medium basin.
A planned 132/33 kV substation project intended to improve supply reliability in Nawalpur and connect back into the Haripur 33/11 kV node through new 33 kV sub-transmission links.

The single largest solar procurement event in Nepal's history. A national-scale competitive-bid tender launched 2024 for 960 MWp of utility-scale grid-tied solar PV across Nepal, awarded to 63 projects from multiple IPPs. PPA signing and financial closure staged over FY 2025/26 a

The utility-scale grid-tied solar programme operated by Nepal Electricity Authority. Sits inside NEA as a distinct programme line that has grown from a single 25 MWp NEA-EPC plant (Bidur/Trishuli, 2022) to a ~1.1 GWp pipeline as of FY 2024/25.
Figure 2.1-2 NEA Organization Chart (as of September 2022)
Energy-specific profile of Nepal for electricity-system context.

pages.t: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP)
pages.e: 400 kV Tower at Koshi River
38 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Nilgiri · in Mustang district.
30 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Nyadi · in Lamjung district.

A bi-national mega-project on the mahakali-basin river forming the Nepal-India border. At 6,480 MW, the largest proposed hydropower project in the region — and the most instructive case study in hydro-geopolitics anywhere in South Asia. Signed into a treaty in 1996, the project h

Phukot Karnali is a 426-480 MW peaking run-of-river project on the Karnali in Kalikot district, promoted by Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited. It is pinned in both the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is one of the few large Karnali p

A proposed eastern Chitwan 132/33 kV substation intended to strengthen supply for the Chitwan load pocket and reduce the dependence on the Bharatpur-side single-circuit connection described in the FY 2019/20 annual book.
37 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Rahughat · in Myagdi district.
40 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Rahughat · in Myagdi district.
120 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Bhote koshi · in Rasuwa district.

Rasuwagadhi is a 111 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Rasuwa district — one of the larger projects in Nepal's under-construction pipeline and a key asset in the Chilime Hydropower group portfolio.

Nepal's planned first grid interconnection with China in the annual-book 2020 planning frame. The project is both a power-trade idea and a geopolitical signal: a proposed 400 kV route from the Ratmate side of the Nepal grid to the Rasuwagadhi/Kerung border axis.

pages.t: Sahas Urja — Solu Khola (Dudh Koshi) Hydroelectric Project
pages.e: 86 MW run-of-river hydropower project on the Solu Khola, a tributary of the Dudh Koshi, in Solukhumbu District, Koshi Province. Nepal's most-cited private IPP success story — and the project that proves the standard PPA rate is only viable at exceptional scale.

Sanjen Khola is a 78 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Sanjen Khola in Rasuwa district. At nearly double the capacity of the adjacent Sanjen project (42.5 MW), it is the larger of the two Sanjen River developments.
Sanjen is a 42.5 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Sanjen River in Rasuwa district, promoted as part of the Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL) subsidiary network.
25 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Singati · in Dolakha district.

The Solu Corridor Tingla-Mirchaiya 132 kV line evacuates generation from the Solu/Okhaldhunga area south toward Mirchaiya. It is operational, but the public route geometry is still lower-confidence than the main 220 kV and 400 kV corridors because the current trace is reconstruct
23.5 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Solu Khola · in Solukhumbu district.

Sun Koshi No.3 is a 536 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Koshi basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it combines large reservoir scale with meaningful dry-season output in Nepal's eastern-central hydro corridor.
49.6 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Dordi · in Lamjung district.
Super Madi is a 44 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Madi Khola in Kaski district, Gandaki Province.
9.6 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Mai Khola · in Ilam district.
7.8 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Mai Khola · in Ilam district.
40.27 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Nyadi · in Lamjung district.
155 MW · survey/study stage · on the Tamor · in Taplejung district.
Super Trishuli is a 100 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Trishuli River — the largest project in the Trishuli cascade's current development pipeline.
350 MW · survey / study · on the Dudhkoshi · in Solukhumbu district.

Tamakoshi 3 is a 650 MW survey-stage project on the Tama Koshi in Dolakha. It is pinned in data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is the sixth-largest capacity marker in the display set and sits in the same high-head eastern hydropower geography as upper-tamakoshi.
99.8 MW · under construction · on the Tama Koshi · in Dolakha district. A cascade project of upper-tamakoshi (456 MW), designed to operate in tandem with Nepal's largest power plant.
Tamor Storage is a proposed 200 MW storage hydropower project on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal, currently at the survey/study stage.

A 140 MW storage-type reservoir scheme on the Seti River — the only large dam-and-reservoir project actually in active construction in Nepal, and therefore the single most important test case for whether the country can execute storage-deficit-closing assets at all. ADB-financed,

A 132/33 kV, 30 MVA substation commissioned as part of the Solu corridor build. In the annual-book snapshot, Tingla is the bridge intended to connect Solu basin IPP output into the larger 132 kV system once the Mirchaiya–Tingla line is complete.

The Trishuli River is Nepal's most heavily developed hydropower corridor, benefiting from proximity to Kathmandu's load center and well-established transmission infrastructure. Starting from the original 1967 Trishuli plant, the corridor has accumulated layers of development acro
75 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Trishuli · in Nuwakot district.
Repair of generator side covers during overhauling Stator of unit-2 during overhauling

The Udipur-Damauli-Bharatpur 220 kV reinforcement is the lower Marsyangdi evacuation route from the Udipur area toward Bharatpur/Aptari. In the map it is represented as the source-backed Udipur-Markichowk-Bharatpur line because that is the route basis stated in the recovered NEA
ERT survey at IkhuwaKhola HPP (IKHPP) Core Drilling at JawaTila
36 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Balephi · in Sindhupalchok district.

Upper Bhotekoshi is a 45 MW operating project on the Bhote Koshi in Sindhupalchok. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist not because it is large, but because it is a useful reality-check marker: an existing high-gradient Koshi-basin plant where the river valley, opera
203 MW · survey / study · on the Budhi Gandaki · in Gorkha district.
22.2 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Chaku · in Sindhupalchok district.
25 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Dordi · in Lamjung district.
38.46 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Kalanga Gad · in Bajhang district.

A 900 MW run-of-river scheme on the Karnali, licensed to India's GMR Group since 2008 and still in survey / pre-construction 18 years later. Upper Karnali is the canonical example of an India-developed Nepali hydropower project that has not been built: repeatedly extended PDA dea
26 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Khudi · in Lamjung district.
52 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Lapche · in Dolakha district.
25 MW · operating · on the Madi Khola · in Kaski district.

Upper Marsyangdi A is a 50 MW operating run-of-river plant on the Marsyangdi River in Lamjung District — the upstream anchor of the 170 MW Marsyangdi cascade. Developed by Sinohydro-Sagarmatha Power Company (a Sino-Nepali joint venture), the plant signed its PPA in 2010 and commi
42 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Modi Khola · in Kaski district.
Figure 6.3-12 River Longitudinal Profile of the Myagdi Khola River
43 MW · under construction (generation licence) · on the Nyasem Khola · in Sindhupalchok district.
Permanent Camp Facility
Upper Tamor is a 285 MW hydropower project under construction on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal — one of the largest private/IPP projects in the under-construction pipeline.

Upper Trishuli-1 is a 216 MW generation-licence / under-construction-stage project on the Trishuli in Rasuwa. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist because it sits in the central-north Trishuli corridor, where hydropower value depends directly on evacuation capacity i
Lifting of upper bracket during overhauling

Upper Trishuli 3B is a 37 MW run-of-river hydropower project in the Trishuli cascade, developed under Trishuli Jal Vidyut Company Limited — a subsidiary corporate model distinct from the NEA's directly-owned Trishuli assets.

Uttarganga is an 828 MW storage hydropower project in Baglung promoted by NEA. It is pinned in the data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects layer because it is the fourth-largest capacity marker in the display set and one of the few top-10 records that is explicitly a storage project.
Nepal's government planning body for water and energy. Produces the authoritative basin plans, potential assessments, and energy balances that form the data backbone of this research.
Figure 6.3-2 River Longitudinal Profile of the West Seti River
The operational 132 kV Terai-side grid chain that carries the map west of Butwal: Butwal-Shivapur-Lamahi-Kohalpur, Kohalpur-Bhurigaun-Lamki, and Lamki-Pahalwanpur-Attariya-Mahendranagar.

Availability Declaration (AD) penalties are financial deductions levied by NEA against hydropower producers when actual generation falls below the availability the producer declared in advance. The AD penalty mechanism is the primary financial instrument through which the nea-tri

Module costs have collapsed, grid costs are middling, LCOE is already below small hydro (solar-lcoe-crossover). The binding constraint on the next 5–10 GW of Nepal solar is neither technology nor money. It is land. Specifically, it is the politics of converting Terai agricultural
This marker is the terminal reach of the generated route-to-ocean trace.
This dependency zone marks the shared Ganges corridor where separate Nepal-linked routes become one downstream political geography.

Buildability is the first-order filter on Nepal’s hydropower future: a project is not “good” because a desk study assigns a large MW number—it is good if the entire delivery package remains credible after geology, hydraulics, hazards, and grid realities are stressed.

What Nepal's electricity consumption growth trajectory implies for the country's energy strategy — the import-displacement opportunity, the growth risks, and the metric that determines whether the hydro build-out succeeds or fails on its own terms.

Domestic-led hydro means planning the power system around Nepal's own load growth, reliability, and productivity — using exports to monetize surplus, not to define the investment program. After the solar audit, the better operational phrase is domestic-led power strategy with a h
This dependency zone gives context for the western and central Nepal-linked routes entering the agricultural plains of eastern Uttar Pradesh.

Energy substitution pathway names the specific question a hydro build-out must answer if it is to transform Nepal's energy system and not just its export ledger: which non-electric energy flows are being displaced, in what order, by what policy and infrastructure? Without this le

The IPSDP executive summary treats environmental flow and protected-area restrictions as buildability constraints, not as side notes. In the SEA mitigation table on page 37, moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows and m

Firm power is dependable, year-round, dispatchable electrical capability under credible drought and system stress assumptions—not a branding label for “big projects,” and not interchangeable with installed megawatts, annual energy (GWh), or even average seasonal output.
This marker explains where the gandaki-basin route enters the Ganges trunk through the Narayani/Gandak downstream system.

Nepal occupies only about 13% of the Ganges basin area, yet contributes roughly 40–45% of mean annual flow and on the order of 70–80% of dry-season flow measured at Farakka-style accounting points in strategic basin literature. This hydrological asymmetry—small footprint, large l
This marker explains the naming and political transition where the lower Ganges route enters Bangladesh and becomes part of the Padma system.
This marker explains the downstream handoff for the karnali-basin route. Nepal's Karnali becomes the Ghaghara system downstream and eventually joins the Ganges trunk.

Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are rapid releases of water from moraine-dammed or ice-dammed lakes, often triggered by avalanches, seismicity, or hydrostatic/overpressure failure. In Nepal’s context, they are a first-order constraint on how confidently one can site run-of-r

Green hydrogen in Nepal is best understood as a bridge technology between surplus monsoon electricity and hard-to-electrify domestic industry—not as a export commodity fantasy disconnected from grid physics. Electrolysis converts low-utilization wet-season kWh into storable molec

pages.t: How To Read The Geopolitics Map
pages.e: The geopolitics preset is not a Nepal-only map. It follows Nepal-linked rivers into the northern Indian plains, Bangladesh, and the Bay of Bengal because the political question is about downstream exposure, not just upstream origin.

Solar plants are not free-floating technology choices; they are three things co-located — a module field, a land parcel, and an interconnection. Cheap modules have collapsed the first; solar-lcoe-crossover. Land and interconnection are now the binding constraints on Nepal solar,

Hydro-geopolitics is the lens that treats Nepal’s rivers as co-produced by geology, monsoon, ice, and downstream human systems—and therefore as objects of bargaining, not merely as engineering inputs. Electricity is one slice of a larger portfolio that includes irrigation, flood

The insurance market for Nepal's hydropower sector — mandatory coverage requirements, premium escalation, claim settlement failures, and the systemic interaction with AD penalties and debt service obligations. The central connection is to nea-triple-authority: when physical damag

“Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power).

How Initial Public Offerings (IPOs) and rights share issuances in Nepal's hydropower sector are used to transfer project debt risk onto retail investors — and why the "Chilime success model" is not replicable.
This marker explains where the koshi-basin route reaches the Ganges trunk after crossing the North Bihar floodplain.
This dependency zone marks the coastal-delta context where river timing, sediment, salinity, and Bay of Bengal exposure meet.
This marker explains where the far-western Nepal-linked route stops being only a border-river story and starts becoming part of the wider downstream Ganges geography.

The NEA Triple-Authority Conflict describes how the Nepal Electricity Authority simultaneously holds three powers over private hydropower producers (IPPs) that, in a well-designed market, would be separated across independent institutions:

In Section 2.4 of moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, the IPSDP does two different but related things. It names three generator-facing wholesale customer categories, and it separately describes the commercial modes those channels may use, including exchange-market sales and long-term

This dependency zone explains why the Kosi and Gandak routes cannot be read as simple blue lines. They pass into one of the most sensitive floodplain and agricultural geographies in the downstream story.
This dependency zone explains why the geopolitics map follows Nepal-linked routes into Bangladesh.
This marker explains where the shared downstream route becomes a lower-delta system rather than a single clean river line.

Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day; it is a multi-decade

How Power Purchase Agreement rates are set for hydropower projects in Nepal — and whether rate-setting follows an economic formula or a discretionary process. The central institutional framework is nea-triple-authority: the same entity that sets PPA rates (NEA as single buyer) is
Live checklist for the Nepal Energy wiki-map explorer. Updated as each item lands. Persists across agent sessions so either of us can pick up where the other stopped.

Q-design discharge is the regulatory parameter that mechanically determines a hydropower project's installed capacity and its Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) contracted energy obligation. A design discharge set too high — based on optimistic or outdated hydrology — inflates both t

The utility-scale solar story (hybrid-siting-logic, agrivoltaics-and-land) is one of three tracks. The other two — behind-the-meter rooftop and off-grid / mini-grid distributed PV — operate under different institutions, different financing, and a different political coalition. Th

Nepal’s power system is dominated by run-of-river (RoR) hydropower: plants that divert part of a river through headworks and a tunnel, then return it downstream without holding a seasonal inventory of water. RoR is not “small hydro” and not automatically low-impact—it is an archi

The seasonal arbitrage trap describes how Nepal can appear to "win" on electricity trade while losing on economic value: the country systematically sells cheap wet-season surplus and buys expensive dry-season power, so headline trade balances mask a destroyed margin on timing.

Seasonal mismatch is the central structural problem of Nepal’s hydropower-heavy system: inflow and demand follow different calendars. It is not a one-off drought story and not primarily a “we need more MW” story—it is what happens when a monsoon river regime meets a comparatively

In Nepal, sediment is often discussed as an operations headache — rakers, flushes, lost efficiency. That understates the issue. Sediment is a core design constraint: it determines what kind of plant you can build, what turbine you can credibly run, how fast maintenance capital is

Nepal's structural problem is timing (claim-timing-not-volume). The single most under-used lever for fixing timing is the one physical resource whose generation profile is a near-mirror of Nepal's RoR hydrograph: utility-scale solar PV.

The single most-out-of-date assumption in Nepal's public energy discourse is that solar is expensive relative to hydro. It was true in 2014. It was marginal in 2019. By the NEA's 2081/82 (2024/25) competitive-bid awards, solar had become the cheapest new-build generation in Nepal
Three sentences to lead with, because they invert most people's priors:

Nepal’s energy crisis is often narrated as a shortage of projects. At the water–energy interface, the sharper truth is often a shortage of seasonal inventory: storage deficit—the gap between the impoundment capacity needed to re-time monsoon water to dry-season scarcity and what

Stranded generation is the condition where plants could produce but the grid cannot absorb or move the energy—so operators spill water, throttle output, or receive dispatch orders to stop. In Nepal, this is increasingly a monsoon phenomenon tied to a simple mismatch: generation b

Ranked bottlenecks for Nepal's hydropower system, synthesized across the source notes, data pages, and system-analysis pages. The ranking carries an important nuance: the answer changes depending on whether you ask about deep structural cause or immediate practical blocker.

The geopolitics preset should answer a larger question than "where do the rivers go?" It should show how Nepal-origin rivers connect Himalayan water, future hydropower buildout, downstream agriculture, flood risk, dry-season scarcity, and regional bargaining power.

This page is a narrow comparison between the state-planning frame in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 and the wiki's higher-level strategic claims. It only records points that the executive summary states clearly enough to compare without guesswork.
The central argument of the Nepal Energy & Hydropower knowledge hub, supported by the source notes, data pages, and synthesis pages linked below.

Condensed notes on how the wiki's argument was assembled and checked. This page is a methodology aid for readers who want to understand which parts of the hub are source summaries, which parts are synthesis, and where confidence is strongest or weakest.

Solar changes the master thesis without replacing it. The original frame - Nepal is failing to convert hydrological advantage into firm, deliverable, resilient, financeable, politically executable power - remains correct. What changes is the conversion machine. It is no longer cr

pages.t: Solar's Role in the Winter Deficit
pages.e: The wiki's existing framing treats Nepal's winter deficit as a storage problem (storage-deficit, seasonal-mismatch). The honest 2026 framing is: part-storage, part-solar, part-demand-shaping. This synthesis is an argument for the updated framing, with quantified contribution shar

This wiki is a public guide to Nepal's electricity system: where power is generated, why the system can have monsoon surplus and winter scarcity at the same time, how transmission and trade shape project value, and why solar, storage, and domestic demand matter alongside hydropow
A rational 20-year water-and-energy strategy for Nepal, synthesized from the strategy and evidence pages.
Areas where public evidence remains thin, source definitions conflict, or more granular data would materially improve the hub.

pages.t: Climate Change Increases Volatility and Resilience Risk, Not a Simple "More Water" Win
pages.e: Climate change does not make Nepal's hydro story straightforwardly easier via a naive "more water" narrative. In plausible futures, it increases volatility, tail risks, and resilience burdens on infrastructure and operations.

pages.t: Nepal Should Prioritize Domestic-Led Hydro With Export Support
pages.e: Nepal should pursue a domestic-led hydro strategy (electrification + productive use + minerals/industry where credible), with exports as support, rather than an export-led strategy that risks treating domestic demand as a residual.

pages.t: Floating PV Is a Leverage Play, Not a Core Supply Pillar
pages.e: In Nepal, floating PV is a high-leverage niche rather than a core national supply pillar: useful on reservoirs and hydropower ponds where it avoids land conflict and shares grid infrastructure, but too limited by reservoir stock to carry the solar build-out.

pages.t: Governance Weakness Wastes Hydro Opportunity Through Delays and Misaligned Incentives
pages.e: Governance weakness is one of the main channels through which Nepal wastes hydro opportunity: time-to-build, cost inflation, mispriced risk, and under-delivery of public goods—even when engineering solutions exist on paper.

pages.t: India Is the Decisive External Actor in Nepal's Hydro Future
pages.e: India is the decisive external actor in Nepal's hydro future: primary market interface, frequent balancing partner, key transit state for regional trade (including Bangladesh pathways), treaty counterpart on shared basins, and a geopolitical filter on certain third-country develo

pages.t: Installed MW, Energy, Seasonality, and Firm Power Are Routinely Conflated
pages.e: Installed megawatts, annual generation, seasonal output shape, and firm (dependable) power are routinely conflated in Nepal's hydro discourse—producing misleading narratives about both "potential" and "solved" energy security.
Peaking run-of-river (PRoR) is not the same as seasonal storage. Treating PRoR as a substitute for multi-month reservoir regulation is a category error with strategic implications.

pages.t: Operating Fleet Is Overwhelmingly RoR/PRoR, Not True Storage
pages.e: Nepal's operating hydro fleet is overwhelmingly run-of-river (RoR) and peaking run-of-river (PRoR), with only a tiny share of true seasonal storage capacity.

pages.t: Sediment Is a Central Design and Economics Constraint, Not an O&M Footnote
pages.e: Sediment is a central hydropower constraint in Nepal—shaping design, outage risk, maintenance cycles, reservoir economics, and rebuild timelines—not a secondary operations-and-maintenance footnote.

pages.t: New Utility Solar Is Now Cheaper Than Small Run-of-River Hydro in Nepal
pages.e: For Nepal's next marginal MW of non-firm generation, utility-scale solar is now cheaper than small run-of-river hydro on a blended tariff basis, and its advantage grows when timing value is counted.

pages.t: Solar's Durable Political Coalition Is Rural and Distributed, Not Just Utility-Scale
pages.e: Nepal's durable solar coalition is not only the utility-scale IPP market. It is also rural and distributed: AEPC programmes, mini-grids, solar pumps, rooftop systems, and off-grid users create the political constituency that keeps solar policy resilient.

pages.t: Terai Solar Is Nepal's Only Large Short-Cycle Build Lever Before 2030
pages.e: For large new supply that can materially affect Nepal's pre-2030 winter balance, Terai and substation-adjacent solar is the only short-cycle build lever at national scale.

pages.t: Storage Is the Clearest Physical Answer to Seasonal Mismatch
pages.e: Seasonal storage (multi-week to multi-month energy shifting) is the clearest physical answer to Nepal's seasonal power mismatch—even though it is not the easiest financial, social, or geopolitical answer.

pages.t: Nepal Fails on Conversion of Hydrological Advantage Into Deliverable Power
pages.e: Nepal is not failing because its rivers are "fake" or because annual water is trivial. It is failing to convert a real hydrological advantage into firm, deliverable, resilient, financeable, and politically executable power and productive demand.

pages.t: Nepal's Core Hydro Problem Is Timing, Not Annual Volume
pages.e: Nepal's core hydro problem is a timing problem (when water arrives and when demand and export windows need power), not a simple annual water-volume problem.

pages.t: Transmission and Delivery May Be the #1 Immediate Monetization Bottleneck
pages.e: Transmission and delivery readiness may be the #1 immediate monetization bottleneck—turning available energy into paid, moved, non-curtailed deliveries—even if seasonal storage remains the deeper physical bottleneck for firm domestic and strategic export shape.

Agricultural energy use in Nepal — small in total volume, but strategically central to food security, diesel displacement, and rural electrification outcomes. Agriculture employs ~60% of the workforce but accounts for only 0.95% of final energy — because most farm work is manual
Consolidated discharge data for Nepal's major river basins. Public sources reference these numbers; provenance varies.

Where Nepal's electricity is consumed — by province, by NEA distribution office, showing the stark development gradient from Terai industrial corridors to remote Karnali valleys. Data from the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25, Distribution and Consumer Services Directorate provincial

Where Nepal's electricity actually goes — by sector, by consumer category, and how fast it's growing. Central evidence for domestic-led-hydro-strategy and the seasonal-arbitrage-trap value-capture argument.

The full fuel × sector matrix of Nepal's final energy consumption, straight from the WECS Energy Sector Synopsis 2024 (FY 2079/80). This is the page that answers "how much of Nepal's total energy is actually hydro?" with a number other than the one everyone quotes. It is the deno
Nepal's installed hydropower fleet, broken down by technology type. The RoR dominance is the structural cause of seasonal-mismatch in generation.
Which industries consume Nepal's electricity — and which ones burn coal and biomass instead. The companion page to data-domestic-demand and the industrial-demand half of energy-substitution-pathway.

Catalog of the merged map stack imported from the code workspace. This page is the bridge between the wiki's analytical pages and the interactive / geospatial asset layer.

This page is the index for the explorer layer-control labels. Each row below links to an individual wiki page with deeper context, interpretation notes, caveats, and related pages. The hover popups in the map use the same labels and open these pages directly.
Nepal's hydropower system benchmarked against relevant mountain/hydro-dominant peers. The comparison exposes what separates "having rivers" from "having a functional hydro economy."

The annual book's appendix gives a rare structured view of both the installed high-voltage transformer base and the next wave of substation expansion under construction. Together, they show why NEA was still in a catch-up phase even as generation was accelerating.

NEA's FY 2076/77 transmission-length table captures the grid just before the full 400 kV build-out visible in later years. It is the cleanest single-book snapshot for the legacy 132/220 kV system plus the first operational 400 kV segment.
Annual System Peak Load Curve
The quantitative companion to solar-resource-geography-nepal. Table-first; map-spec at the end.
Reference page for the copied scripts and the data products they generate. The scripts now live in the merged workspace under , but not all raw dependencies were copied over.
The reduction from theoretical potential to actually installed capacity. The 28x gap is not primarily a resource failure — it is a systems failure.

pages.t: Rooftop and Solar Home System Deployment
pages.e: The volume-and-geography companion to rooftop-minigrid-offgrid. The numbers for the two solar tracks that never appear on NEA installed-capacity tables but together move ~700 GWh/year across more than a million households.

The utility-scale solar analogue of data-fleet-composition. The full "what is on the ground, what has PPA, what is being built, what is tendered" picture for Nepal solar — the frame for every capacity-planning argument in the wiki.
The hour-by-hour and month-by-month numbers that make solar-hydro-complementarity visible. Two figures should make this profile visible as time curves.
The numerical table behind solar-lcoe-crossover. Three time series — global solar LCOE, Nepal solar auction tariff, Nepal hydro PPA — plus a value-weighted adjustment.
Nepal's water storage infrastructure compared to regional and global peers. This is the single most dramatic infrastructure gap in the entire analysis.
Nepal's electricity trade transformation from net importer to seasonal exporter. Central evidence for seasonal-arbitrage-trap and seasonal-mismatch.
Design and implement a coordinated cooking electrification programme with four integrated components:
Separate the Nepal Electricity Authority's three conflicting roles into independent institutions through three specific legal/regulatory actions:

Issue a formal directive from the Department of Electricity Development (DoED) mandating that all new hydropower project feasibility studies submitted for licensing must incorporate climate-adjusted hydrological decrement factors based on the ICIMOD "Guideline for Climate Resilie
This intervention has two parallel tracks:
Complete two transmission infrastructure priorities with specific mechanisms:
